title: After-sun aloe lotion
subtitle: A cooling, water-light lotion with aloe vera gel and lavender essential oil
excerpt: Aloe vera gel is the active cooling ingredient here. Emulsifying wax binds the small amount of fractionated coconut oil into a light lotion that absorbs quickly. Lavender essential oil soothes and adds fragrance. A preservative is required because this formula contains water.
sourceNotes: After-sun lotion formulation from Gracefruit cosmetic-making guide (gracefruit.com, May 2026). Aloe vera gel properties from supplier datasheet. Preservative usage from Cosmetic Formulation, Perry (2015). Lavender EO from Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014).

recipe.batchNotes: Scale proportionally. Keep all percentages exactly the same: particularly the preservative at 1% of total batch weight.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Lasts up to 3 months refrigerated. Apply generously to sun-exposed skin as needed.
recipe.temperatureNote: Melt the emulsifying wax into the oil at 70 degrees Celsius. Heat the aloe vera gel separately to 70 degrees Celsius. Combine the two phases at matching temperatures for a stable emulsion.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 months refrigerated with the preservative at 1% of batch weight. The aloe vera gel is the stability-limiting ingredient. Discard if the texture separates or the lotion develops an off smell. No preservative, no shelf life: do not make without Optiphen Plus or an equivalent.

recipeTools.notes: Or any heat-proof jug; dedicated to cosmetic use

glossaryTerms.definition: A solid emulsifier that binds oil and water into a stable lotion or cream. Used at 4 to 8% of total formula weight. Common options include BTMS-50, Polawax, and E-wax NF. Each has slightly different usage rates: follow the supplier's recommendation.

A cosmetic preservative effective against bacteria and fungi. Required in any formula that contains water. Optiphen Plus (Phenoxyethanol + Caprylyl Glycol) is the standard beginner option at 1% of total batch weight. Add below 40 degrees Celsius. Do not substitute food-grade acids.

content.text: This lotion uses a hot-process emulsion method. The oil phase (emulsifying wax and fractionated coconut oil) and the water phase (aloe vera gel) are heated separately to the same temperature and then combined. The
forms a stable bridge between the two phases. The result is a light, fast-absorbing lotion rather than a heavy cream.

is non-negotiable in any water-containing formula. Bacteria and fungi grow readily in the moist, nutrient-rich environment of an emulsion. Optiphen Plus at 1% is effective and well-tolerated by most skin types. Add it when the lotion has cooled to below 40 degrees Celsius.

items.prepNote: Stabilised cosmetic aloe vera gel; the water phase
5% of batch; binds the oil and water phases
1% of batch; Optiphen Plus or equivalent; add below 40°C
1% dilution; add with the preservative below 40°C

content.text: Heat the aloe vera gel in a bowl over a simmering-water bath to 70 degrees Celsius. In a separate jug, melt the emulsifying wax into the fractionated coconut oil over the same water bath, also to 70 degrees Celsius. Pour the oil phase slowly into the water phase while stirring continuously. Continue stirring as the emulsion forms and cools. When the lotion has cooled below 40 degrees Celsius, stir in the preservative and the lavender essential oil. Pour into a clean bottle or pump dispenser. Refrigerate.

3 months refrigerated. This formula keeps for a shorter time than oil-only preparations because the water phase supports microbial growth even with a preservative. Discard if the lotion changes colour, texture, or smell. Do not use on broken or sunburnt skin that has open blisters.
